ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for lemongrass as a food ingredient (FOODON_03311737) but no specific entry for lemongrass juice or brewed lemongrass beverage; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers herbal infusions and similar beverages; no specific lemongrass juice code exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for lemongrass juice beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one cup (250 ml / 250 g), as typically served in a restaurant glass at Din Tai Fung Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Lemongrass tea / lemongrass infusion, brewed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for lemongrass-based beverages
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for commercially brewed lemongrass juice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for brewed herbal teas (lemongrass infusion) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA lemongrass beverage composition data. The Din Tai Fung version is described as a brewed drink with ice cubes made from the same beverage, suggesting a lightly sweetened lemongrass infusion. A modest sugar contribution (~3–5 g/100 ml) is included to reflect typical commercial preparation. Macros are low given the predominantly water-based nature of the drink.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of herbal teas and lightly sweetened botanical beverages — analogous estimate based on lightly sweetened herbal infusion data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for lemongrass juice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 40 (low category) based on analogy with lightly sweetened herbal infusions and botanical teas, which have low GI values due to minimal rapidly digestible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (40 / 100) × 3.4 g carbohydrate × 2.5 = approximately 3.4 g, which is low. This estimate is consistent with the predominantly water-based, lightly sweetened nature of the beverage.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~2 mg/100 ml) and potassium presence (~72 mg/100 ml)
hypertension.guidance: Lemongrass juice is very low in sodium and provides modest potassium, making it a suitable beverage choice for individuals managing hypertension; however, patients should confirm the sugar content of the commercial preparation and limit total daily sugar intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and low glycemic load per serving (~3.4 g), with modest added sugar (~3.2 g/100 ml)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lemongrass juice has a low estimated glycemic index and low glycemic load per serving, posing minimal acute glycemic risk for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, portion size and any added sugar in the commercial preparation should be confirmed, as larger servings will increase total sugar and carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and saturated fat content; low sodium; lemongrass contains citral and antioxidant compounds
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lemongrass juice contains negligible fat, saturated fat, and sodium, and lemongrass (Cymbopogon citratus) is associated with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties in preclinical studies; it is a heart-friendly beverage choice when consumed without excessive added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemongrass Juice (香茅汁, Jus Serai) is a brewed herbal beverage made by infusing fresh or dried lemongrass stalks (Cymbopogon citratus) in hot water, then chilling the resulting liquid. It is a popular non-alcoholic drink in Singapore and Southeast Asia, served at Chinese restaurants including Din Tai Fung, where it is marketed as a signature house beverage. The drink has a pale yellow to light green colour, a refreshing citrus-herbal aroma, and a mildly sweet, slightly tangy flavour with characteristic lemongrass fragrance. The Din Tai Fung version is notable for using ice cubes made from the same lemongrass beverage to preserve flavour consistency as the drink dilutes. Nutritionally, lemongrass juice is a low-calorie, predominantly water-based beverage with approximately 14 kcal per 100 ml, minimal protein and fat, and modest carbohydrate from light sweetening (~3.4 g/100 ml). It has a low estimated glycemic index (~40) and low glycemic load per serving, making it suitable for most dietary patterns including those managing Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease. Lemongrass contains bioactive compounds including citral, geraniol, and flavonoids with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties documented in preclinical research. The beverage is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal-suitable. It is commonly served as a cold drink accompaniment to dim sum, noodle dishes, and other Chinese restaurant meals in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lemongrass juice?
Is lemongrass juice good for diabetics?
Din Tai Fung lemongrass drink calories
meal_context: cold drink with Chinese restaurant meals
signature house beverage at Din Tai Fung Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for commercially brewed lemongrass juice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central herbal tea / lemongrass infusion data and FAO/INFOODS SEA reference data, with a modest sugar adjustment for commercial preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for lightly sweetened herbal infusions. Confidence is medium rather than low because the food is a simple water-based herbal infusion with well-understood compositional characteristics.
notes: Fat_g is naturally zero as lemongrass juice is a water-based herbal infusion containing no fat by nature. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as the lemongrass solids are not consumed in the strained beverage. Vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based beverage with no animal-derived components. Vitamin_d_mcg is naturally zero as lemongrass juice contains no dietary vitamin D. Sentinel value -1 is used for vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg as these micronutrients are not reported in the USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases for lemongrass infusion beverages. Glycemic index set to 40 (low category) as an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for lightly sweetened herbal infusions; no direct GI measurement for lemongrass juice is available. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (40/100) × 3.4 g × 2.5 = 3.4 g (low). The Din Tai Fung version is priced at SGD 7.41 per serving at Marina Bay Link Mall as of the field survey data data capture date. Follow-up: HPB Singapore Foodo should be rechecked periodically for a direct lemongrass juice entry that would allow upgrade to high confidence.

improvement_actions_taken: Assigned canonical English food_name 'Lemongrass Juice' replacing promotional menu description.
Added Chinese simplified/traditional (香茅汁), Malay (Jus Serai), Tamil (லெமன்கிராஸ் ஜூஸ்), and Hindi (लेमनग्रास जूस) local names to satisfy G6 Singaporean cuisine multilingual requirement.
Estimated nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central herbal tea data and FAO/INFOODS SEA reference, with honest estimated source_match_type.
Set fat_g=0.0 with zero-macro justification phrase 'naturally zero' in notes.
Set glycemic_index=40 (positive numeric) per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule; gi_category='low'; GL calculated per serving.
Used FoodOn closest_parent and FoodEx2 closest_parent with gap notes; SNOMED CT set to none with gap note.
Verified all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases list.

Updated the justification for all -1 sentinel values in the 'notes' field to explicitly name the source databases (USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA) for improved clarity and compliance with the g3_numeric_hygiene rule.
